<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 I'm noticing a discrepancy between the dashboard formatting and what the PDF extraction expects. The DashboardView uses `formatPounds` for display, but the tech spec requires the PDF to use `formatMoney` with the full currency and country code parameters. This creates a problem because the assertion in T73 expects every money string from the dashboard to appear verbatim in the extracted PDF text, but they're being formatted differently. So the test fixture itself uses `formatMoney`, and T73 is checking that every money string visible on the dashboard matches what appears in the PDF text. If the dashboard uses `formatPounds` and the PDF uses `formatMoney`, they'll produce different outputs for whole-pound amounts — `£1,650` versus `£1,650.00` — which would cause the test to fail. I need to either change the dashboard to use `formatMoney`, change the PDF to use `formatPounds`, or accept that the strings won't match. Let me calculate what Jordan's actual values would produce with each formatter to see if there's any way they could align. The test plan expects `DashboardView` to accept a `snapshot` prop directly and use `formatMoney` for all money strings, but the current implementation takes different props and uses `formatPounds` instead. The test fixture defines `formattedJordanDisposable` as the output of `formatMoney`, so the PDF extraction will only match if the dashboard renders that exact same formatted string. Looking at Jordan's actual numbers, formatPounds produces whole pounds like `£1,650` while formatMoney would output `£1,650.00` — they don't match, so T73 can't pass as written. This is a genuine design gap: the tech spec says the PDF uses formatMoney, but the dashboard uses formatPounds, and the test requires them to be identical. I need to route this back to /tech-spec rather than guess which formatter should win. and the migration belongs in S12 since that's where we're reconciling the two surfaces. T73 is the test that validates this reconciliation, so updating DashboardView to use formatMoney falls within S12's scope — it's the code path required to make T73 pass.

Actually, let me reconsider the substring matching: if the dashboard renders `£1,650` and the PDF contains `£1,650.00`, the test would find `£1,650` as a substring within that. So technically the test might pass without changing DashboardView at all.

But there's a complication with negative numbers — the dashboard uses the Unicode minus sign `−£280` while `Intl.NumberFormat` produces an ASCII minus `-£280.00`, so that substring wouldn't match. I need to verify what `toLocaleString` actually returns for negative values in the en-GB locale.

The dashboard's `formatSignedDisposable` function explicitly adds the U+2212 minus sign before the formatted amount, so to match that in the PDF, I should format the disposable the same way rather than relying on `formatMoney` directly. For T73 specifically, the test is checking income and expenditure totals which are positive, so the sign issue only matters for the disposable amount. The tech spec says to use `formatMoney`, but I might need to handle the signed disposable specially to ensure the PDF string matches what appears on the dashboard. I see there's a helper function that formats signed money with the U+2212 minus sign for negatives, producing `−£280.00`. The real question is whether T73's assertion uses substring matching or exact layout matching — if it's just checking for presence of the money strings, then the current approach might work, but I need to verify what the test actually expects.

Looking at the test more carefully, it lists three money values that should appear in the PDF: the disposable amount, income total, and expenditure total. The disposable is already defined as a formatMoney fixture, so the PDF needs to output that exact value. For the income and expenditure totals, the test would pull them from the rendered dashboard and verify they're in the PDF text.

If the dashboard uses formatPounds (producing `£1,650`) and the PDF uses formatMoney (producing `£1,650.00`), a substring check would still pass since `£1,650` appears within `£1,650.00`. But for the disposable, the dashboard currently outputs something different than the fixture, so I need to figure out which approach keeps things in scope.

The cleanest solution is to have the PDF use formatMoney directly on all three values without any signed-money helper. This gives the exact fixture value for disposable and matches what the test expects, while the dashboard's substring remains valid for income and expenditure. So the three values all check out: the disposable amount matches the formatMoney output, and both the income and expenditure totals from the dashboard appear as substrings in their PDF equivalents. No changes needed to DashboardView.

Now I need to install @react-pdf/renderer and verify it's set up correctly. The package isn't in node_modules yet, so I'll need to pin an exact version and then check that renderToBuffer is properly exported and returns a Promise<Buffer>. I should also review what the previous decisions documented about version pinning in S020 and S021.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 I'm realizing the DashboardView component has a specific props interface that doesn't match what I'm passing in. It expects `outcome`, `copy`, `delta`, `latestSnapshotId`, and `personaLabel`, but I added a `snapshot` prop that it doesn't actually accept. I need to figure out what the component's API should look like after this migration, especially since it needs access to `formatMoney` with the full set of parameters like `pence`, `currency`, and `countryCode`.

The dashboard page already has the latest snapshot available, but there's a fallback case where no snapshot exists — it creates a default outcome with empty data. For that scenario, there's no snapshot to pull currency and country code from. I could either pass those as separate props with defaults, or make `snapshot` optional on DashboardView and handle the null case gracefully. Since the financial summary gets hidden when there's no snapshot anyway, I can make the snapshot optional and rely on it being present whenever money actually needs formatting.

Looking at the test case T73, it expects DashboardView to accept a `snapshot` prop directly. Rather than completely refactoring the component signature, I'll add `snapshot` as an optional prop alongside the existing ones — that's less disruptive and lets me extract currency and country code from it when available.</details></think>

 The existing tests rely on `buildDashboardProps()` to construct props, and `DashboardViewProps` is already exported. To support the new requirement, I could add optional `currency` and `countryCode` props that default to GBP and GB respectively, so current tests remain unaffected, then use these in the `formatMoney` calls. Alternatively, I'm considering a simpler approach: add an optional `snapshot` prop that the View can reference when provided.

Looking at the actual dashboard page structure, I see that `latest` snapshot might be undefined in the no-data persona case, but the financial summary only renders when there's actual data, so the formatter would only run when the snapshot exists and its currency/countryCode are available. I think the cleanest solution is to add optional `currency` and `countryCode` props with sensible defaults, then have the dashboard page pass them from the latest snapshot when available.

Actually, I'm reconsidering the test plan wording for T73 — it says `<DashboardView snapshot={snapshot} />`, but that's more about the intent (testing money-string parity) than the exact API contract. Since refactoring DashboardView to accept a snapshot prop would touch all the S4 tests, I'll stick with option 2: keep the existing DashboardView signature and just add the currency/countryCode props with defaults. <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 Most tests are passing now, but I've got two specific failures to fix. The first is that DashboardView is still calling `formatPounds` which strips decimal places, but the test wants the full `£1,650.00` format — I need to migrate that over. The second issue is trickier: I can't spy on `fs.writeFileSync` in ESM modules the usual way, so I'll need to rethink how I'm mocking the file system. `fs.promises.writeFile` can be spied on since it's a configurable object property, whereas `writeFileSync` is trickier in ESM. The real issue is that `renderSnapshotPdfToBuffer` runs for real in the test, so I need to verify that `@react-pdf/renderer`'s `renderToBuffer` doesn't write temp files to disk — it should accumulate everything in memory via pdfkit.

The test plan explicitly requires spying on both `fs.promises.writeFile` and `fs.writeFileSync`, so I could use a `vi.mock` wrapper around the fs module to replace `writeFileSync` with a spy function, though I need to be careful since the mocked db module might depend on other fs methods like `mkdirSync`. I'm considering whether to use `vi.spyOn` on just the promises version since the PDF renderer is async anyway, or to mock the entire `node:fs` module with a selective override. The cleanest approach might be using `vi.hoisted` with `vi.mock` to replace the module entirely in the test environment.</details></think>

## Summary

`/implement S12` is complete. Third (final) stretch slice shipped per tech-spec §S12 (rev 5.1) with T68–T75.

### `@react-pdf/renderer` verification (S020 D-144 + S021 D-152, four sub-checks closed)

1. **Exact pin**: `"@react-pdf/renderer": "4.5.1"` in `package.json` (no caret).
2. **`renderToBuffer` exported from package root**: `node_modules/@react-pdf/renderer/index.d.ts:790` declares `(element: React.ReactElement<DocumentProps>) => Promise<Buffer>`; `lib/react-pdf.js:367` confirms it in the export list.
3. **No Chromium binary**: `node_modules/.bin/` carries zero Chromium / Chrome / Playwright / Puppeteer binaries; `node_modules/@react-pdf/*` totals 3.0 MB.
4. **Pure-Node rasterisation**: `lib/react-pdf.js:320` shows `renderToBuffer = element => renderToStream(element).then(stream => new Promise(...))` — no `child_process`, no `puppeteer.launch`.

### What landed

- **Library wrappers** — `lib/pdf/SnapshotPdf.tsx` (12-section React-PDF document), `lib/pdf/render.tsx` (`renderSnapshotPdfToBuffer` delegation).
- **Route Handler** — `src/app/(main)/dashboard/snapshot/[id]/pdf/route.ts` with `runtime='nodejs'`, persona+ownership gates ordered before render, three response headers, lifecycle `pdf: rendered` log line.
- **Download placement** — `components/DownloadPdfLink.tsx` rendered inside `<DashboardView />` (latest snapshot) and per-row in `<HistoryList />`.
- **Deferred MVP migration** — `formatPounds → formatMoney` across `<DashboardView />` + `<HistoryList />` (per S024 D-226 handoff; required by T73's cross-surface no-drift guard).
- **Fixtures + helper** — `snapshotJordanStoredGbp` / `snapshotPatStoredGbp` / `formattedJordanDisposable` in `tests/_fixtures/snapshots.ts`; `pdfTextExtractor` in `tests/_helpers/pdfText.ts` via `pdf-parse@2.4.5`.
- **Tests T68–T75** — 14 new test cases across 8 files under `tests/s12/`.

### Verifications

- `npm test` → **236 / 236** (was 222 → +14 new S12 cases).
- `npm run lint` → clean.
- `npm run typecheck` → clean.
- `npm run build` → clean; new route `ƒ /dashboard/snapshot/[id]/pdf` server-rendered on demand.

### Recorded for follow-up (not patched in this slice)

- `/tech-spec` round needed for: three queued S019 amendments; carve-out clarification for `formatPounds` in calculator `reasons[]` and `formatPenceForInput` in `<UpdateForm />`; drizzle-kit/Biome meta-JSON workflow.
- `/test-plan` round needed for: T73 prop-shape reword (additive `currency` / `countryCode` props instead of `snapshot={snapshot}`); T75 mock-shape reword (`vi.mock('node:fs', ...)` instead of `vi.spyOn(fs, 'writeFileSync')` — D-238).
- Full audit trail in [S025-implement-s12.md](docs/ai/sessions/S025-implement-s12.md) and the new `PROMPT_HISTORY.md` row.

No PRD / tech-spec / test-plan edits in this slice. All three stretch slices (S10 / S11 / S12) are now delivered.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

All clean — lint, typecheck, and 236 / 236 tests pass.

### What moved

In `components/DashboardView.tsx`, inside the hero `<section aria-labelledby="snapshot-heading">`:

1. **`<ShareSnapshotForm />`** now sits **above** the Dashboard actions nav (rendered only when `latestSnapshotId !== null`), wrapped in a `mt-6` div so it gets the same spacing it had before.
2. **`<DownloadPdfLink />`** is now **inside** the `<nav aria-label="Dashboard actions">`, slotted between "View past submissions" and the primary "Update / Add my income & outgoings" CTA (rendered only when `latestSnapshotId !== null`, matching the existing no-data carve-out for "View past submissions").

The standalone `{latestSnapshotId !== null && <div className="mt-6 flex flex-col gap-3">…</div>}` block that used to live below the Reasons/Delta grid is removed — both children now live up in the hero.

### Why the `latestSnapshotId !== null` guards

The previous block guarded both pieces together with `latestSnapshotId !== null` (because both need a snapshot id to do anything useful). Splitting them into the new positions keeps that exact gate per piece:

- **ShareSnapshotForm** — needs the snapshot id to mint the link, so it's only rendered when one exists.
- **DownloadPdfLink** — the PDF Route Handler returns 404 for a nonexistent snapshot id, so the link is only rendered when a snapshot exists. This also keeps the new-customer (no-data) state clean: no "View past submissions" + no "Download PDF" + the CTA reads "Add my income & outgoings" instead of "Update".

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 Looking at the D-235 document, I'm seeing that the rationale about Calculator's `reasons[]` and formatPounds being deferred is actually incorrect — the critic has confirmed this. The issue is that for whole-pound amounts, formatPounds behaves differently than formatMoney, which contradicts the original assumption that they'd produce byte-identical output for the MVP's single currency.

The visible regression is clear: on the dashboard, headline numbers use formatMoney with consistent 2-decimal formatting while reasons[] uses formatPounds which strips trailing zeros, creating an inconsistency. To fix this in F1.1, I need to migrate the calculator's reasons[] to use formatMoney instead, which requires either passing currency and countryCode into the assess() function itself, or moving the formatting responsibility to the consumer side. Option 1 is cleaner since reasons are built once at assess time, though it does change the public API. Looking at how assess() is currently called in the snapshot schema, it doesn't receive currency or countryCode information, so I'd need to either propagate those parameters through the function signature or defer the formatting until render time based on each snapshot's currency settings. The real issue is that `assess()` only receives the income entry, not the currency or country code from the snapshot, so reasons get hardcoded with £ at calculation time. The cleanest architectural fix would be to pass currency and countryCode as parameters to `assess()` so it can use `formatMoney()` properly, then update all the call sites that invoke it.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 Changing the `assess()` signature would require updates across multiple files—the calculator itself, the database snapshots and seed modules, and a couple of action handlers in the dashboard. That's a significant refactor footprint to consider.

The workflow guidance suggests routing this back through a `/tech-spec` round to clarify whether `assess()` should accept currency and country code parameters, since those values aren't currently available at call time. But there's a simpler alternative: make `formatPounds` an alias of `formatMoney` with GBP and GB defaults, which would eliminate the visible drift without changing the function signature. The real issue is ensuring the calculator's reason strings match what `formatMoney` produces, and there are a few different approaches to solve that.

The cleanest option is to update `formatPounds` to always output two decimal places, matching the GBP/GB output from `formatMoney` — it's a one-line fix with no signature changes and the calculator stays pure domain logic. The risk is minimal since existing tests use `toContain` rather than asserting exact string matches. Alternatively, I could have the calculator use `formatMoney` directly with hardcoded GBP/GB, or remove `formatPounds` entirely and import `formatMoney` instead, though those feel less elegant. The deeper architectural question is whether the calculator should remain currency-agnostic or accept currency parameters, but for now the MVP only ships GBP, so making `formatPounds` produce identical output to `formatMoney` with those defaults solves the immediate problem. Refactoring `formatPounds` to delegate to `formatMoney` with hardcoded GBP/GB parameters is the surgical fix — it closes the visible drift, eliminates the `pence / 100` violation, keeps the calculator currency-agnostic, and requires only a one-line change without expanding scope. So I'll implement Option C: make `formatPounds` delegate to `formatMoney` with GBP and GB locale, which aligns the calculator's output with the dashboard while keeping the helper in place and satisfying the pence-division rule. Then I need to update the session snapshot and run the full test suite.

For the remaining fixes, I'm adding file system operations to T75's spy set and `formatMoney` output strings to T74's forbidden array—both are test-file-only changes. F1.4 and F1.5 are optional refinements, but since the critic flagged F1.1 through F1.3 as in-slice fixes, I should apply those three at minimum.

Now I need to check whether HistoryList can actually render with zero snapshots, or if each row is always backed by a snapshot. If there's no empty-state scenario, the guard the critic mentioned isn't necessary.</details></think>

Critic round closed cleanly. Here's the summary.

## [Critic verdict on S12 implementation](e24db630-2643-4aa9-b24e-f6842e65c497): Minor fixes — closed

The critic ran read-only against the S12 slice (PDF route handler, `<SnapshotPdf />`, T68–T75 tests) plus the rolled-in `formatPounds → formatMoney` MVP-surface migration. Verdict: **Minor fixes** — 1 real in-slice regression, 2 test-coverage gaps, 1 cheap nit, plus optional/route-back items.

### Applied in-slice

| # | Severity | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| **F1.1** | Blocker | `formatPounds(pence)` now delegates to `formatMoney(pence, 'GBP', 'GB')` in `lib/affordability/format.ts`. Closes the visible drift where the same dashboard surface showed `Disposable income: +£50.00` (headline, `formatMoney`) alongside `you have £50 left this month.` (reason, `formatPounds`). Calculator's currency-agnostic `assess(ie)` signature preserved — no ripple through the ~30 call sites; the S023 D-213 source-discipline rule ("no `*Pence / 100` outside `formatMoney`") now also holds for `formatPounds`. |
| **F1.2** | Should-fix | `tests/s12/t75-no-file-written.test.ts` spy set widened to cover `fs.createWriteStream`, `fs.appendFileSync` (via `vi.mock`), and `fs.promises.appendFile` (via `vi.spyOn`) in addition to the original `writeFileSync` / `promises.writeFile`. The natural "save copy to disk" regression class (`createWriteStream(path).end(buffer)`) no longer slips through. |
| **F1.3** | Should-fix | `tests/s12/t74-logging-hygiene.test.ts` forbidden list extended with the three `formatMoney(...)` output strings (income, expenditure, abs disposable). A regression that emitted the formatted £ string would have slipped past the digit-substring search. |
| **F1.4** | Nit | `tests/s12/t70-route-cross-persona-404.test.ts` asserts `Content-Type` / `Content-Disposition` / `Cache-Control` parity across the cross-persona and missing-id 404 arms. Side-channel distinguishability between the two arms is now testable. |

### Routed back (not patched inline)

- **F3.1** — D-235's "byte-identical for GBP / GB" claim corrected in place in `docs/ai/sessions/S025-implement-s12.md`; new "Post-critic fixes (S025.1)" section records F1.1–F1.4 with cite-back to the original wrong claim so the audit trail survives. PROMPT_HISTORY row extended with the same close-out. Session-snapshot history, not the spec — no `/tech-spec` round needed.
- **F3.3** — TEST_PLAN T73 (prop-shape wording) and T75 (`vi.spyOn(fs, 'writeFileSync')` wording) reconciliation remains on the handoff list as a `/test-plan` candidate.

### Declined

- **F1.5** — dedicated `<DownloadPdfLink />` render / a11y `T*` and HistoryList empty-state guard. The HistoryList empty-state branch already returns before rendering any `SnapshotRow`, so the link can never appear with zero snapshots. Adding a dedicated `T*` would extend T68–T75 scope; the link is covered transitively by T34 / T39 axe smoke + the cross-cutting SC 2.5.8 via `min-h-10`.

### Verifications

- `npm test` → **236 / 236** across 79 files (same total — F1.1 was internal helper aliasing; F1.2 / F1.3 / F1.4 strengthened existing tests without adding new ones)
- `npm run lint` → clean (Biome 2.2.0)
- `npm run typecheck` → clean

### Files touched (post-critic)

```13:35:lib/affordability/format.ts
/**
 * Locale-aware money formatter for outcome surfaces. The `pence / 100` divide
 * is display-only; all affordability arithmetic upstream stays in integer
 * pence (per tech-spec §S10 integer-pence invariant).
 */
export function formatMoney(
  pence: Money,
  currency: Currency,
  countryCode: CountryCode,
): string {
  const locale = localeByCountryCode[countryCode];
  return new Intl.NumberFormat(locale, {
    style: "currency",
    currency,
  }).format(pence / 100);
}

/**
 * GBP-denominated £ string for currency-agnostic call sites (the affordability
 * calculator's `reasons[]`, which doesn't carry a currency / countryCode and
 * pre-MVP is GBP-only). Delegates to `formatMoney(_, 'GBP', 'GB')` so dashboard
 * headline numbers and reason strings stay byte-for-byte identical, and the
 * tech-spec §S10 source-discipline rule ("no `*Pence / 100` outside
 * `formatMoney`") holds for this helper too.
 */
export function formatPounds(pence: Money): string {
  return formatMoney(pence, "GBP", "GB");
}
```

- `tests/s12/t70-route-cross-persona-404.test.ts` — header parity assertions
- `tests/s12/t74-logging-hygiene.test.ts` — formatted-money forbidden tokens
- `tests/s12/t75-no-file-written.test.ts` — extended spy set (5 file-write surfaces)
- `docs/ai/sessions/S025-implement-s12.md` — D-235 corrected + "Post-critic fixes" section
- `docs/PROMPT_HISTORY.md` — S025 row extended with S025.1 close-out

The slice is now ready to commit when you're ready. The two carry-forwards (formatPounds carve-out clarification in tech-spec §S10, T73 / T75 wording reconciliation in test-plan) are non-blocking and remain queued on the handoff list.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
 Considering whether moving the PDF download link from the bottom navigation to next to the headline makes sense from a UX and accessibility angle—the current setup has the snapshot tag, status indicator, headline, and metric cards flowing down the hero section, with the download action tucked into the nav at the bottom.

The main tension is that placing a download button right next to the headline competes for visual attention and shifts the page's tone from reflective (which the PRD emphasizes) to transactional. The headline is the emotional core—"You have some room after your outgoings"—and a button there undermines that. Plus, grouping the download action with the other dashboard actions in the nav makes semantic sense for screen readers, and separating it would create redundancy. There's also a layout challenge: the headline spans full width, so fitting a button beside it on desktop either requires wrapping them together or dealing with awkward text wrapping. I should give them a clear recommendation with reasoning rather than just making the change, then offer concrete alternatives they can choose from—like reordering the nav to put Download PDF first, or keeping it where it is. This is a design decision they should make consciously after understanding the trade-offs, especially since they just asked me to move it into the nav in the first place.</details></think>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7)**_

Honest answer: **no, I'd push back on that move.** Here's the reasoning before you decide.

## What's at stake

You'd be putting a 154 × 40 px secondary action button immediately to the right of the page's most important element — the reflection headline (`"You have some room after your outgoings"`). That has a few costs:

### 1. It demotes the headline
The headline is the emotional centre of the dashboard per the tech-spec §S4 bullet 2 — it's the line that delivers R20's "reflection-not-advice" framing in one sentence. Pairing it with a download button visually re-frames the page as "here's a thing, now save it" rather than "here's where you stand". Reviewers reading the brief's "vulnerable-customer-first" lens will feel that drift.

### 2. It breaks the actions grouping
The current `<nav aria-label="Dashboard actions">` groups three peers — *View past submissions*, *Update my income & outgoings*, *Download PDF* — under a single landmark. Lifting one out means:
- Screen-reader users encounter the download as a stray link **inside** the heading region rather than under the actions landmark, then never hear it announced in the nav.
- The "Dashboard actions" nav becomes a 2-item nav, weaker as a landmark.

### 3. It fights the responsive layout
The headline runs `text-2xl ... sm:text-3xl` full-width inside the hero section. To put a button to its right you'd have to either:
- Wrap them in `flex justify-between` — the no-data headline (`"We don't have any income or outgoings to assess yet."`) would wrap awkwardly around the button on narrow widths.
- Hide the button on mobile (then mobile users lose the download entirely on the surface where you wanted it most prominent).

### 4. PDF download is a low-frequency, Could-class stretch action
R13 is a Could under PRD §3; the button doesn't need prime real estate next to the headline. The hero's primary affordance is already the *Update my income & outgoings* CTA with the `ArrowRight` accent. Promoting Download PDF to headline-adjacent makes a secondary stretch feature read as primary.

### 5. Brand-mark proximity
The `↑ Surplus` band chip already sits just above the headline. Adding a button to the headline's right gives that small area three competing things (band chip, headline, button) — the band chip is supposed to be the at-a-glance signal and gets crowded.

## What I'd suggest instead

If the goal is "make Download PDF easier to find", there are lower-cost ways than headline-adjacent placement.

Done. **236 / 236, lint + typecheck clean.**

### New per-row layout

```
<li> <!-- history row -->
  ... date · outcome state label ...
  ... disposable line · band chip ...

  <!-- disclosure (preview) + download (save) — personal-consumption pair -->
  <div class="flex flex-wrap items-start justify-between">
    <details>
      <summary>Show what was submitted on 11 June 2026</summary>
      <IeBreakdown />  <!-- expands below, inside the details column -->
    </details>
    <DownloadPdfLink />  <!-- pinned top-right via items-start -->
  </div>

  <!-- outbound action -->
  <ShareSnapshotForm />
</li>
```

### Why `items-start`

The `<details>` is a block element whose height varies (collapsed = ~24 px, expanded = full IE grid). Anchoring the cross-axis with `items-start` keeps the button aligned to the summary row when the disclosure expands, so the button doesn't visually drift downward beside the income/outgoings grid.

### Note on cross-surface consistency

The two surfaces now intentionally diverge:

- **Dashboard** — no inline disclosure → `<DownloadPdfLink />` pairs with `<ShareSnapshotForm />` (both are "snapshot outputs").
- **History row** — has an inline disclosure → `<DownloadPdfLink />` pairs with the disclosure (preview / save pair); `<ShareSnapshotForm />` lives separately as the outbound action.

That's defensible because the surfaces have different content structures, but if you'd rather keep them symmetric (either Disclosure + Download on both, or Share + Download on both) say the word and I'll align them.

Done. **236 / 236, lint + typecheck clean.**

### New Dashboard layout

```
<section> <!-- hero -->
  ... snapshot tag · band chip ...
  ... greeting ...

  <!-- headline + download (top-right) -->
  <div class="flex flex-wrap items-start justify-between gap-3">
    <h1>You have some room after your outgoings</h1>
    <DownloadPdfLink />     <!-- only when latestSnapshotId !== null -->
  </div>

  ... metric cards · irregular-income note ...

  <ShareSnapshotForm />     <!-- only when latestSnapshotId !== null -->

  <nav aria-label="Dashboard actions">
    View past submissions
    Update my income & outgoings
  </nav>
</section>
```

### Layout notes

- **`items-start`** keeps the download button anchored to the top of the headline row, so if the headline ever wraps to two lines (longer copy on smaller viewports), the button doesn't drift downward.
- **`flex-wrap`** means on narrow viewports the button wraps below the headline rather than squeezing it.
- **Conditional render preserved** — the button only appears when `latestSnapshotId !== null`, so the no-data persona never sees an orphaned export link.
- **Headline `id={SNAPSHOT_HEADING_ID}` preserved** — the `<section aria-labelledby="snapshot-heading">` landmark still resolves correctly.
- **Existing tests pass** because none asserted on the headline's parent shape; the headline itself still carries its semantic role and id.

### One trailing inconsistency to flag

Dashboard and History rows now have different Download PDF homes:
- **Dashboard** — next to the headline (top-right of hero).
- **History row** — next to the disclosure summary ("Show what was submitted on...").

That's defensible (each surface places it next to its most prominent per-snapshot anchor — headline on Dashboard, disclosure on History), but it's worth knowing in case you want to push toward symmetry later.
